paragraphs.text: “And the waters were diminished after the hundred and fifty days. And the ark rested… upon the mountains of Ararat… and in the tenth month… were the tops of the mountains seen… And it came to pass in the six hundredth and first year… in the first day of the first month, the waters were dried up from off the earth… And in the second month, which is Iyar, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, was the earth dried” (Gen 8:3–5, 13–14). So Noah and those with him were in the ark three hundred and sixty-five days. For from the twenty-seventh day of the second month, Iyar, to the twenty-seventh day of the same month of the next year, by the lunar reckoning of months, three hundred and sixty-five days passed. See then, even in Noah’s generation they counted in the year three hundred and sixty-five days; how then can it be asserted that the Chaldeans and Egyptians invented and established such a reckoning?

“And the Lord God spoke to Noah… Come out of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons’ wives with thee” (Gen 8:15–16). Into the ark God brought them in separately, that they might keep purity; out of it He brings them in pairs, that they may grow and multiply on the earth. The animals too kept purity in the ark, as is shown by the words: “bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, and every flesh… and they shall breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth” (Gen 8:17).

When Noah had come out of the ark with all who were with him, “he took of every clean beast… and offered burnt offerings on the altar” (Gen 8:20). On the day Noah came out of the ark, all the clean birds and beasts obeyed Noah; he offered every clean flesh as a sacrifice pleasing to God, and this sacrifice put an end to the Flood. “And the Lord smelled” (Gen 8:21) not the savor of the flesh of animals or the smoke of wood, but He looked and saw the purity of heart in the one who out of everything and on behalf of everything offered Him sacrifice. And the Lord God said to Noah what he wished to hear: “For thy righteousness’ sake the remnant of the creatures has been preserved and did not perish in the waters of the Flood; and for thy sacrifice’s sake, offered of every flesh and for every flesh, I will not again bring a flood upon the earth.” God as it were binds Himself beforehand with a promise, that He may not again bring a flood upon men if they should again walk after the wicked imagination set on evil every day.

And since by the Flood sowing and harvest had been interrupted, and the order of seasons disturbed, God restores to the earth what had been taken from it in wrath, and says: “While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease” (Gen 8:22). For during the Flood, during forty days because of the rains, there was night; and throughout the year, until the earth dried, winter continued without summer.
